schools.summary: Paul opens his last letter with his apostolic title and its ground: 'an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God' — his office resting not on human appointment but on the will of God who called him; 'according to the promise of life (the promise of eternal life) which is in Christ Jesus' — his apostleship in the service of the promise of life, the gospel he proclaims being the good news of life in Christ, fitting for a letter written facing death (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).
Chrysostom: Paul, awaiting martyrdom, opens with the promise of life — the life that death cannot touch, held out in Christ; the dying apostle's first word is of life, his comfort and his theme.

themes: Paul, an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God
according to the promise of life which is in Christ Jesus
an apostle by God's will
the promise of life in Christ

schools.summary: The tender address: 'to Timothy, my dearly beloved (agapeto — beloved) son' — Paul writes to his spiritual child and closest companion with deep affection, Timothy a son in the faith begotten through the gospel; the threefold blessing 'grace, mercy, and peace' (fuller than the usual 'grace and peace') adds 'mercy' to grace (free favour) and peace (its fruit), fitting for a young minister needing God's compassion and help in his trials; 'from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Lord' — Father and Son the joint source, a witness to Christ's divine dignity (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: To Timothy, my dearly beloved son
Grace, mercy, and peace, from God the Father and Christ Jesus our Lord
the address to his beloved son
grace, mercy, and peace

schools.summary: Paul opens with thanks: 'I thank God, whom I serve from my forefathers (in the line of his pious ancestors, as a worshipper of the God of Israel) with pure conscience' — he serves the same God his fathers served, with a clear conscience, the gospel being the fulfilment not the abandonment of his fathers' faith; 'that without ceasing I have remembrance (mention) of thee in my prayers night and day' — Timothy continually in Paul's prayers, the aged apostle even in prison keeping up unceasing intercession for his beloved son (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

themes: I thank God, whom I serve from my forefathers with pure conscience
without ceasing I have remembrance of thee in my prayers night and day
Paul's constant thankful remembrance of Timothy
unceasing intercession

schools.summary: Paul's deep desire for reunion: 'greatly desiring to see thee' — the aged, imprisoned apostle longed to see his beloved son once more before his death, the yearning of a father for his child; 'being mindful of thy tears' — remembering the tears Timothy had shed, probably at their last parting, the memory of that grief deepening his longing; 'that I may be filled with joy' — the reunion would turn his sorrow to joy, the sight of Timothy a source of deep gladness (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott, Wesley).

themes: Greatly desiring to see thee, being mindful of thy tears
that I may be filled with joy
Paul's longing to see Timothy
the memory of his tears and the hope of joy

schools.summary: Paul's recollection: 'the unfeigned (anhypokritos — sincere, without hypocrisy, genuine) faith that is in thee' — Timothy's faith real and unfeigned, not a pretence; 'which dwelt first in thy grandmother Lois, and thy mother Eunice' — a godly ancestry, the faith passed down through his grandmother and mother (Jewish believers, Acts 16:1), the influence of pious women in his upbringing; 'and I am persuaded that in thee also' — Paul confident the same genuine faith now dwelt in Timothy, the heritage of faith having borne fruit in the son (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: the unfeigned faith that is in thee, which dwelt first in thy grandmother Lois, and thy mother Eunice
I am persuaded that in thee also
Timothy's unfeigned faith inherited through Lois and Eunice
a godly heritage of faith

schools.summary: Paul's charge: 'stir up (anazopyrein — rekindle, fan into flame, as one stirs a dying fire to a blaze) the gift of God which is in thee' — Timothy to rouse and rekindle the spiritual gift God gave him, that it not grow cold or dim through neglect; the 'gift (charisma)' the spiritual endowment for ministry, to be exercised and kept aflame not left idle; 'by the putting on of my hands' — given at Timothy's ordination, when Paul (with the presbytery, 1 Timothy 4:14) laid hands on him, the solemn occasion of his commissioning (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: that thou stir up the gift of God, which is in thee by the putting on of my hands
the charge to stir up the gift
rekindle, fan into flame
the gift given at ordination

schools.summary: The ground for stirring up the gift and for boldness: 'for God hath not given us the spirit of fear (deilias — cowardice, timidity)' — the Spirit God gives is not one of cowardly fear, timidity in ministry not being from God; 'but of power (dynamis — strength, courage, efficacy), and of love (agape — love to God and men), and of a sound mind (sophronismos — self-discipline, sober-mindedness, sound judgment)' — the threefold gift equipping for faithful ministry: strength to act and endure, love that animates and softens, and a disciplined, self-controlled spirit (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley).

themes: God hath not given us the spirit of fear; but of power, and of love, and of a sound mind
the spirit God gives: power, love, and a sound mind
not cowardice but courage
the threefold gift for ministry

schools.summary: Paul's charge (drawn from the Spirit of power, v.7): 'be not thou ashamed of the testimony (the witness, the gospel) of our Lord, nor of me his prisoner' — Timothy not to be ashamed of the gospel, nor of Paul, now a prisoner for it, the chains no disgrace; 'but be thou partaker of the afflictions (synkakopatheson — suffer hardship together with) of the gospel according to the power of God' — rather than being ashamed, to share in the sufferings the gospel brings, sustained by God's power (Barnes, Robertson).

Chrysostom (with Calvin): Paul calls himself 'his prisoner' — the prisoner of the Lord, not of Nero; his bonds were for Christ, an honour not a shame, and Timothy must not be ashamed of them but glory in them.

themes: Be not thou therefore ashamed of the testimony of our Lord, nor of me his prisoner
be thou partaker of the afflictions of the gospel according to the power of God
not ashamed but sharing the gospel's afflictions
Christ's prisoner, not Nero's

schools.summary: The description of the God whose power sustains: he 'hath saved us, and called us with an holy calling (a calling to holiness, a holy summons)' — both saving and calling, the calling holy in source, nature, and end; the great antithesis 'not according to our works (not earned, not for any merit of ours) but according to his own purpose (his eternal design) and grace (free favour), which was given us in Christ Jesus before the world began (before times eternal)' — the purpose of grace eternal, formed in Christ before time (Barnes, Robertson, Wesley).

The Reformed press this as the foundation of salvation by grace alone, grounded in God's eternal purpose and choice in Christ, excluding all human merit; the believer's salvation rests not on works but on grace given in Christ before the world began.

themes: who hath saved us, and called us with an holy calling, not according to our works
but according to his own purpose and grace, which was given us in Christ Jesus before the world began
salvation by God's purpose and grace, not works
eternal grace in Christ

schools.summary: The eternal purpose of grace (v.9), long hidden, is 'now made manifest (revealed, brought to light) by the appearing (epiphaneia — the manifestation, the first advent) of our Saviour Jesus Christ' — what was purposed before time now disclosed in Christ's coming; 'who hath abolished (katargesantos — brought to nought, rendered powerless, annulled) death' — by his death and resurrection breaking death's dominion and sting for the believer; 'and hath brought life and immortality (aphtharsian — incorruption) to light through the gospel' — illumining and making clear the eternal, incorruptible life dimly seen before (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: Christ has brought to light the certainty of life and incorruption through the gospel; what the saints of old saw only dimly, the appearing of the Saviour has now made plain — eternal life unveiled.

themes: the appearing of our Saviour Jesus Christ, who hath abolished death
hath brought life and immortality to light through the gospel
Christ abolishing death and bringing life to light
the manifestation of eternal grace

schools.summary: Paul's account of his commission for the gospel just described: 'whereunto (for which gospel) I am appointed (placed, ordained) a preacher (kerux — herald proclaiming the message), and an apostle (one commissioned and sent with authority), and a teacher (one instructing in the truth) of the Gentiles' — three terms for his one office in the service of the gospel (the same as 1 Timothy 2:7); his distinctive calling being especially 'of the Gentiles,' the gospel of life brought through him to the Gentile world (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: Whereunto I am appointed a preacher, and an apostle, and a teacher of the Gentiles
Paul's threefold office
herald, apostle, teacher
the calling to the Gentiles

schools.summary: Paul, suffering for his apostleship ('these things' = his imprisonment), is not ashamed, 'for I know whom I have believed (trusted, and still trust)' — not merely what but whom, his confidence resting on personal knowledge of Christ (or the Father) whose power and faithfulness he had proved; 'and am persuaded that he is able to keep (guard, as in a bank) that which I have committed unto him (ten paratheken mou — my deposit) against that day' (the day of Christ's coming). Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson, with Wesley, take 'my deposit' as Paul's own soul and eternal interests, entrusted to God's keeping (cf. Psalm 31:5, 'into thy hand I commend my spirit') — the most precious treasure a man can place in God's safe-keeping.

Clarke notes the diversity of views on 'that which I have committed unto him': some take it as his life (committed to Christ to be received again at the resurrection); others as his soul (given to his faithful Creator, which men may kill but cannot destroy); others as the gospel itself, which God will carefully preserve and proclaim by others whom he raises up — the passive sense (what God committed to Paul), linking to v.14. Wesley: 'my soul.'

debates.question: What is 'that which I have committed unto him' (my deposit)?

sides.position: Paul's own soul and eternal interests, entrusted to God's keeping (Psalm 31:5)
The gospel/ministry God committed to Paul, which God will preserve through others
holders: one view noted by Clarke (cf. v.14)

sides.position: Paul's life, committed to Christ to be received again at the resurrection

themes: I know whom I have believed, and am persuaded that he is able to keep that which I have committed unto him
nevertheless I am not ashamed
Paul's confidence that Christ will keep his deposit
the crux of the deposit

schools.summary: Paul's charge: 'hold fast (echo — retain, keep as a pattern) the form (hypotyposis — outline, pattern, model) of sound (hygiainonton — healthful, wholesome) words which thou hast heard of me' — Timothy to keep the apostolic teaching, the body of sound doctrine, as a fixed pattern, not departing from it; 'in faith and love which is in Christ Jesus' — held not as a dead, formal orthodoxy but in living faith and love, truth and grace together (Barnes, Robertson).

Chrysostom (with Calvin): the 'form of sound words' is the summary of healthful apostolic teaching Timothy received from Paul; he is to hold it as a model and standard, guarding it against the unsound babblings of error.

themes: Hold fast the form of sound words, which thou hast heard of me
in faith and love which is in Christ Jesus
the charge to hold fast sound words
sound doctrine in faith and love

schools.summary: The parallel to v.12: 'that good thing which was committed unto thee (ten kalen paratheken — the good deposit)' — here clearly the deposit God committed to Timothy: the gospel, the sound doctrine, the trust of the ministry, the precious treasure entrusted to his keeping; 'keep (phylaxon — guard, watch over, as a treasure against robbery)' — Timothy to guard the deposit of truth, keeping it pure and unimpaired, defending it against the corruptions of error (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: the keeping is 'by the Holy Ghost which dwelleth in us' — not by human strength alone but by the indwelling Spirit; the Spirit who dwells in believers enables the guarding of the truth against all assault.

themes: That good thing which was committed unto thee keep by the Holy Ghost which dwelleth in us
the charge to keep the good deposit
guarding the gospel by the Spirit
the deposit of truth

schools.summary: Paul's sorrowful report: 'all they which are in Asia (the Asian Christians at Rome, or the Asian believers generally) be turned away from me' — many who should have stood with him had deserted him, 'all' probably meaning the general body not every individual, especially in his hour of trial; they turned away likely out of fear, ashamed of Paul's chains (the very thing he charged Timothy not to be, v.8), withdrawing their support when it became dangerous to be associated with him; 'of whom are Phygellus and Hermogenes' — two named (otherwise unknown), singled out as prominent or especially disappointing examples (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

themes: all they which are in Asia be turned away from me; of whom are Phygellus and Hermogenes
the defection of those in Asia
deserting Paul, ashamed of his chains
the loneliness of the apostle

schools.summary: Paul's grateful prayer for his faithful friend's household: 'the Lord give mercy unto the house (the family) of Onesiphorus' — blessing the whole family of the one who had stood by him; 'for he oft (often, many times) refreshed (anepsyxe — revived, cheered, gave relief to) me' — repeatedly bringing comfort and relief to the imprisoned apostle; 'and was not ashamed of my chain' — unlike those in Asia who turned away (v.15), Onesiphorus was not ashamed to be associated with the prisoner, braving the danger and disgrace to stand by Paul (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: The Lord give mercy unto the house of Onesiphorus; for he oft refreshed me
and was not ashamed of my chain
the faithfulness of Onesiphorus
not ashamed of Paul's chain

schools.summary: The measure of his devotion: 'but, when he was in Rome, he sought me out very diligently (spoudaioteron — earnestly, with great effort), and found me' — Onesiphorus did not let Paul's imprisonment hide him but searched hard until he located the apostle; to find a single prisoner in the vast city of Rome required real effort and persistence, and the danger of seeking out a man accused before Nero made the search the more courageous; his love overcame both difficulty and danger (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: when he was in Rome, he sought me out very diligently, and found me
Onesiphorus seeking Paul out diligently
searching for the prisoner in vast Rome
love overcoming difficulty and danger

schools.summary: Paul's prayer for his friend: 'the Lord grant unto him that he may find mercy of the Lord in that day (the day of judgment, Christ's coming)' — that Onesiphorus, who showed mercy to Paul, may himself find mercy from the Lord at the last day, mercy answering mercy, the highest blessing prayed upon the one who had been merciful to him; 'and in how many things he ministered (served, helped) unto me at Ephesus, thou knowest very well' — his kindness not new but a long record of faithful ministry, at Ephesus as at Rome, as Timothy well knew (Barnes, Robertson, Ellicott).

themes: The Lord grant unto him that he may find mercy of the Lord in that day
in how many things he ministered unto me at Ephesus, thou knowest very well
the prayer that Onesiphorus find mercy
mercy answering mercy
